SENT BY:FACSIMILE AT 2/1/90, RECEIVED 2/1/90; 12:32 8:58 PM; 3-0 SGA 2

FEDERAL ELECTION COMMISSION
WASHINGTON DC 20463

March 1, 1990

CERTIFIED MAIL
RETURN RECEIPT REQUESTED

ADVISORY OPINION 1990-1

Scott D. Roberts, President and
Chief Executive Officer
Digital Corrections Corporation
4152 Blue Heron Boulevard West
Suite 108
Riviera Beach, FL 33404

Dear Mr. Roberts:

This responds to your letter dated December 8, 1989, as
supplemented by your letters dated January 3 and 5,
1990, requesting an advisory opinion on behalf of Digital
Corrections Corporation ("DCC") concerning application of the
Federal Election Campaign Act of 1971, as amended ("the
Act"), and Commission regulations to the use of 900 line
telephone services to promote candidates and political
committees.

You state that DCC, a Florida corporation, is engaged in
providing "900/700 inbound telephone services" to various
entities. Such services allow individuals to dial into an
organization after receiving the program or a line included on the caller's
phone bill. The fee is set by the entities for whom the
service is provided. You note that the appropriate local
and national circuit are provided pursuant to agreements
with AT&T and Cable & Wireless Communications, Inc. You wish
to make 900 line telephone services available to candidates
for Federal offices or to political committees that
financially support their campaigns.

Unlike the "listener only" capability provided by a number
of [illegible]

We note that we have not indicated that any 1992 or other
Presidential campaign may prohibit to contract with DCC, this
advisory opinion does not address the separate and additional
issues raised by 26 U.S.C. § [illegible] and related
Commission regulations at 11 CFR Parts 9031-9039.

AO 1950-1
Page 2

of companies providing PCI Line service, your service allows
the caller to register a response by pressing buttons on a
touch tone telephone in an even response with a rotary phone.
Each program will entail a voice message to the caller and a
statement as to the number of calls. Each call will have
the opportunity to hang up and their no charge, and those
callers remaining on the line will be informed that a receipt
will be forwarded within thirty days for the cost of the
call. The price per call will be fixed for each campaign
with a range of $6 to $50. There will be no additional
contribution added on to the billed amount.

You indicate that DCC is able to identify callers to
ensure proper recordkeeping and to avoid corporate
contributions. A feature used by DCC known as Automatic
Number Identification identifies the exact telephone number
from which the call was placed. The caller will be requested
to provide name, address, and mutual security number, and,
upon completion of the call, DCC can cross-reference the call
to identify the person in which name the phone number is
listed. DCC would then continue assigns to be sent to the
caller and provide detailed number information to the
appropriate committee or candidate, including the caller's
name, address, phone number, and total individual
contribution per caller. This is able to identify the caller
further through the use of voice verification. The voice
can be digitally recorded and printed, thus enabling voice
verification. You state that there will be billing only for
calls placed from verified, correct residential telephones
and that all other calls are not regarded. Thus, there
will be billing for a call in the name and address of the
caller do not match with the name and address of the
listed user. You state that upon receipt of the telephone
bill, each caller is permitted by law to elect not to pay the
fee one time.

You state that each campaign will be purchasing this
service from DCC in its direct and normal charge with no
discrimination between regional and national campaigns.
According to your letters and a sample contract sent with the
January 2 letter, each campaign will pay a deposit for the
programming charges, initial handling of a 900 number, ongoing
monthly utilization of the changed number, and bad debt
reserve. The sample contract provides for a deposit of
$2,000 and states that, if the usage that precedes are less
than the deposits, then shall induce campaign and request

2/ You state that ascertaining the difference between a
residential phone and a business phone is relatively
straightforward. All telephone companies have different
exchange codes for business phones and residential phones.
In addition, there are computer systems that can verify whether a
phone is a private phone listed for residential use.

AO 1890-1
Page 3

either an additional deposit or at FCC's discretion commence
with the termination of the 900 program. You state that
each campaign is "wholly liable" for the costs associated
with the 900 program and that "the up front deposit will
always be adequate to cover any losses."

You state that neither the campaign nor FCC will get
paid until the telephone company carries involved have
received payment on the telephone bills sent to callers.
Recording to the sample contract the net proceeds that the
campaign receives shall be determined by "[the difference
between the FCC [cut off] rate, bills and operating costs,
telephone costs, direct-dialed tolls, and the amount charged to
the calling party." The net proceeds shall be payable to
each campaign within ten days of the time FCC received payment from
the common carriers answering to your letter as a
sample contract, telephone companies impose a hold back
percentage that is never returned from five to ten percent of the total
calls made, and this percentage is held indefinitely for
disregarded calls; that the [illegible] calling party wishes not
to follow through, and FCC [...] and is adjusted based upon
results.

The 900 number will be published by each campaign
through various print and broadcast media, at the sole cost
of the campaign. You state that FCC will not pay a sponsor
fee to any campaign.

Contribution from FCC

The Act prohibits a corporation from making any
contribution or expenditure in connection with any Federal
election. 2 U.S.C. §441b); 11 CFR 114.2(b). The term
"contribution" is expenditure" is defined to include "any
director indirect payment, loan, advance, deposit of
money, or any services, or anything of value...to any
candidate, campaign committee, political party or
organization, in connection with any [Federal] election."
2 U.S.C. §431(b)(1)(i); 11 CFR 114.1(g)(2). "Anything of
value" includes services provided at less than the usual and
normal charge, i.e., less than the commercially reasonable
hourly or agreed-upon work charges for like services prevailing at the
time the services were rendered. 11 CFR 100.7(a)(1)(iii)(B),
Advisory Opinion AO-27 and 15-9-35. You present a
situation in which FCC is attempting to ensure that the
campaign or committee paying for the provision of 900
answering does not receive any services for which FCC remains
uncompensated or to compensating below the usual and normal
charge for all the services provided.

1/ The Commission assumes that the term "bad debt" excludes
those calls for which payment is not made after billing.

AO 1990-1
Page 4

The Commission has considered a number of situations in
which the vendor providing green or services is not paid
directly by the political committee but is instead
compensated wholly or in part by payments from third parties.
In Advisory Opinion 1978-5, a candidate committee authorized
a corporation to produce and market t-shirts bearing the
candidate's name. Apart from distributing order forms, the
committee would incur no expense but would receive one dollar
from the outgoing for every shirt sale. The Commission
concluded that because the company paid all the expenses for
producing and marketing the shirts and would receive a
percentage of value from the bought, in effect, the
corporation would be advancing funds to assist the committee
in fundraising. Advisory Opinion 1988-36 addressed
Opinion 1979-56 involved an agreement between a direct mail
fundraising company and a principal campaign committee
whereby the company would use direct mail fundraising
techniques to obtain contributions for the committee. Under
the agreement, the contributions would be deposited
in the committee's account and three quarters would be
designated per return to the company to sever the company's
costs and to help en a monthly basis. One quarter of the
proceeds would be available for other committee uses. The
company would incur initial expenses in preparing and mailing
the solicitation material. If, during the initial
testing period, the company determined that the program was
less successful than anticipated, the funds raised would
be available to the company. The Commission concluded that
the amount expanded by the company would not be
contributions, or the assumption that the proposed agreement
represented an ordinary mode of operation within the direct
mail industry and that the company's charges were normal for
the services rendered.

The Commission concludes that the proposed transactions
would not constitute an impermissible corporate contribution.
In the situation presented, BOC proposes to require that the
committee pay a deposit which you will receive in order
to cover all the costs associated with the 900 line program
and adequate to cover any losses. You state that each
campaign is solely liable for the quite of the program. You
also state that, if the program in complete failure, it
will be terminated in order to ensure that losses do not
exceed the initial deposits. Your proposal is, therefore,
consistent with the Commission's conclusion in Advisory
Opinion 1979-36.

You have stated that why 900 proceeds going to the
committee and a be determined by the difference between costs
(including the costs of dispatched calls) and the amount
charged to the dialier users. The Commission assumes that
included in the calculation of BOC's costs will be an amount
providing profit to the outgoing on its fees for services
provided, so that monies are provided at the usual and

AG 1990-1
Page 5

normal charge, rather than an outright, 11 CFR
100.7(a)(1)(iv)(A). See Advisory Opinions 1988-17 and
1980-42. The Commission notes that the telephone companies
will hold sback indefmitey a percentage from the amounts
transferred to or in order to amount for disgorged calls,
calls where the calling party will not to follow through,
and bad debt. The Commission minutes that DCC will be
observed trying the telephoring companies the usual and normal
charges for the account and advances provided to it. Thus,
the transaction will not involve an impermissible
contribution resulting from reduced charges from these
companies that ultimately permit greater net proceeds to go
to the committee. For clarification Advisory Opinion 1989-28.4/

Contributions from Callers

In addition to the substantial benefit for DCC, the
acknowledged purpose of the proposed activity is to use
funds for the candidates of member slates purchasing DCC's
services. Under Commission regulations, the entire
amount paid to attend a fundraiser or other political event
and the entire amount paid at the purchase price for a
fundraising item sold by a political committee is a
contribution. 11 CFR 100.7(h)(2). In this situation, the
callers purchase access to a fundraising and political event
conducted through telephone communications and attend a
political committee that has contracted with DCC to provide
a political communication services. The caller makes a
contribution to obtain account, which the committee makes
expenditures to DCC in order to convey its message and then
obtain its fundraising proceeds. Therefore, as in the case
with monies paid in any political fundraising venture or
political committee, the amount paid by the caller, not just

4/ You indicate that the amount paid by the committee and
the receipts from the phone will should be audited and
that, if not, DCC will ask for an additional deposit or
terminate the program. It is conceivable, however, that, in
the course of deciding to terminate or continue a marginal
program, you may make an additional incidental payment that
for a period of time, [same language] or by the payments involved. In Advisory Opinion 1989-21,
non-committee recourse of the vendor after proceeds from
that contribution was based upon a determination that any
advances by the vendor in that presumption were contributions.
the Commission concluded that such additional
payments would be extensions of credit in the ordinary course
of business ensuring that one is within a reasonable
period of time. 11 CFR 113.1(g).

AC 1990-1
Page 6

the net proceed to the campaign, if a contribution subject
to the limitations and prohibitions of the Act. See Advisory
Opinion 1989-3, 1988-47 at 3, 1982-24, and 1970-2.

The Commission assumed, given your statement that there
will be billing only for calls from verified residential
phones, that calls from settlement users such as corporations
or labor organizations will not be charged to the candidate's
bills and, therefore, will not be paid by the user. The Act
also prohibits contributions from foreign nationals.
U.S.C. #441e 11 CFR 110.4(m). Therefore, DCC should also
apply its screening program to prevent billing for telephone
numbers outside the United States.5/

DCC as Agent of the Committee

As a participant in the solicitation of contributions
and the recipient of attributions, DCC is an agent of the
contributing committee. In light of certain obligations on
DCC and the committees, you inquired that the 900 line
number screen would be published in each campaign
committee at the sole cost of the committee. You should
advise your clients that the print advertisements or
broadcast publishing the program should contain a
disclaimer stating the name of the committee authorizing the
activity and the person of entity paying for the
solicitation. 2 U.S.C. #441d(1) 11 CFR 110.11(1). The
ads should notify potential callers that they will be
contributed under the Act is order to help avert
contributions from prohibited sources. See Advisory Opinion
1980-42. In addition, since the payments by the callers are
contributions to the committee, DCC must obtain and forward
to the respective committees the information necessary to
comply with the recordkeeping and reporting requirements of
the Act. This includes the name and address of every person
whose previous contributions to a particular committee in the
same calendar year aggated in any 900, together with the
date and full amount of the pursuant 900 line contribution,
as well as their occupation and name of employer.
2 U.S.C.
#432(c)(1) and (2)(i) and 49(h)(3)(A) 11 CFR 102.8(a)(2),
and 104.3(a)(4)(1)(v).6/ See Advisory Opinion 1980-99 with
respect to recordkeeping for contributions of $50 or less.

5/ The Commission notes the possibility of calls being made
from foreign nationals residing in areas bordering the United
States that receive U.H. broadcast media and that have access
to U.S. telephone connections.

6/ For contributions in excess of $10 and up to $200, a
record of the same information must be kept minus the
occupation and employer's name. 2 U.S.C. #432(c)(1) and
102.9(a)(1),

AO 1990-1
Page 7

You have described in detail the ability of DCC to
identify individuals, collect and report callers and to
require that certain the receipt of corporate and other
prohibited contributions. In regards, however, what DCC will
not receive the proceeds from the telephone company, i.e.,
contribution funds until weeks or, perhaps, months after a
call is made, and there is an indication that, when proceeds
are received, the individual contributions received will be
separately identified. In order to ensure proper
recordkeeping and reporting as to the contributions received,
you should contact the telephone company to provide the names
of the individuals who placed and are included in the
proceeds.1/ If the telephone company is unable to provide
such information to DCC, the proposed may not be implemented.
Such information is necessary for the committee to report
correctly as to the date and receipt of the contributions.
This information is also unmailed to ensure the timely
return of any prohibited or unwelcome contributions not
discovered by your organization and recordkeeping processes.
See 11 CFR 303.1(b).2/

You will also need to inform the committee of the
amounts retained by DCC from proceeds for its costs and fees,
as well as the amounts remitted by the telephone companies.
Such amounts are operating expenses of the committee and
are reportable by it pursuant to the Act. 2 U.S.C.
§432(c)(1)(B) and 438(b)(5)(A); 11 CFR 102.9(b) and 104.3(b)(3)
and (4).

It appears from your memoranda that the funds sent to DCC
by the telephone company, which funds from the callers'
payments of their telephone bills, will be deposited into a
DCC account one. After deduction of DCC's costs and fees, the
remainder is sent to the committee. Such funds, however, may
not be commingled with the funds of DCC, which is a

1/ The Commission notes that the caller will not be stating
his or her employer or occupation when making the phone call.
In light of the contribution range proposed by you, a caller
may make contributions to the committee in contributions aggregating
In excess of $20 to a committee. In addition, a caller who
aggregates above $200 by making a call, DCC, therefore, may
wish to amend its vague request for contributor information
by asking for the callers' employer and occupation or arrange
with the committee, upon receipt of the proceeds, to have the
appropriate contributions unloaded.

2/ The Commission notes that, since the entire payment by the
caller is a contribution to the committee, the amount
returned to the contributor, whether by the committee or DCC,
the amount in excess of the applicable limit.

AD 1980-1
page 8

corporation. See 2 U.S.C. [audit(h)] compare 2 U.S.C.
432(h)(5); 11 CFR 104.6. None of the proceeds, therefore,
may be deposited in any of the DCC's existing accounts, but
will need to establish a separate bank account with respect
to each committee in a bank or depository institution
designated by the commission. 11 CFR 103.2(a). The proceeds must be deposited in the
appropriate designated account; i.e., the account of the
committee designated, within 10 days of DCC's receipt.
11 CFR 103.3(a). See Advisory Opinions 1989-21 and 1980-42.
As stated above, the disbursement made from the proceeds for
NRCC's own and fees and the amount retained by the
telephone companies will be offsetting expenditures of the
committee.

The Commission notes that the sample contract contains a
clause stating that the DCC shall have the rights to endorse
itself in any duct of the agreement to an affiliated
marketing entity. Steps should be taken by you to ensure
that any signee or licensee complying with the requirements
set cut in this opinion.

This response constitutes an advisory opinion concerning
application of the Act or regulations prescribed by the
Commission to the specific transaction or activity set forth
in your request. See 2 U.S.C. 437f.

Sincerely,

[Signature]

Lee Ann Elliott
Chairman for the
Federal Election Commission

SUBJECT:  Use of 900 Numbers in Fund Raising
Our File No. 25157/83U

Ms. Kathrine Donovan
General Counsel
California Fair Political
Practices Commission
428 J Street, Suite 800
P. O. Box 807
Sacramento, California  95804-0807

Dear Ms. Donovan:

This is a request for advice pursuant to Government Code
section 83114(b).  It concerns the legality of directly or
indirectly raising money for Daniel E. Lungren, a candidate for
California Attorney General, by use of a 900 number.

The Lungren campaign is proposing to run a television
commercial, for example, a 30-second spot.  A visual or audible
message would appear at the end of the commercial inviting callers
to call a 900 number for further information concerning the
Lungren campaign.  A disclaimer would also run notifying the
caller that this is a toll call and will cost the caller money.

DIEPENBROCK, WULF, PLANT & HANNEGAN

Ms. Kathrine Donovan
March 23, 1990
Page - 2 -

Once the caller calls the 900 number, they will be presented with

several options.

Option A will contain a message thanking the caller for

calling and asking if they would like to hear Mr. Lungren's

position on a variety of issues.  If the caller chose to do so,

the caller could select a message by way of the corresponding

number and listen to the message.  The caller will incur a charge

of, for example, $2.00 for the first minute and $1.00 for every

minute thereafter.  The telephone company would receive a share of

that money as a user fee.  The remaining money would result in a

"profit" to the Lungren Campaign.  The cost to the caller begins

to run immediately after the call is answered.

Is this a legal way to raise money under the Political Reform

Act?  If it is, must the Campaign include in its disclaimer,

either to run on the television commercial or during the message,

that a portion of the cost of the call will be considered a

contribution to the Lungren for Attorney General Campaign?

Option B also encompasses the scenario outlined in Option A,

but includes asking the caller if he/she would like to contribute

to the Lungren for Attorney General Campaign.  The contributor

would then press the corresponding number for contributions, in

addition to the phone charges, and a contribution to the Campaign

would be made.

Several questions arise under this scenario.  May the

contribution be placed directly on the caller's phone bill, to be

DIEPENBROCK, WULFF, PLANT & HANNEGAN

Ms. Kathrine Donovan
March 23, 1990
Page - 3 -

collected through the phone company?  May the phone operator

obtain a VISA or Master Charge number and charge the contributor

via his or her credit card?  May the Campaign use a tele-network

to collect the money raised?  There are costs attendant to each

collection effort that will reduce the total contribution

ultimately received by the Campaign Committee.

The Campaign Committee does not foresee problems in keeping

track of the indirect and direct contributions, as that

information is, according to the phone company, easily tracked.

The Committee is seriously considering this idea, and will

appreciate your prompt advice.

If you have further questions, please call me at 444-3910.

Thank you.

Very truly yours,

DIEPENBROCK, WULFF, PLANT
& HANNEGAN

By [signature]
TRACEY S. BUCK-WALSH

DIEPENBROCK, WULFF, PLANT & HANNEGAN

embedding.qa_text: **CERTIFIED MAIL RETURN RECEIPT REQUESTED** **ADVISORY OPINION 1990-1** Scott D. Roberts, President and Chief Executive Officer Digital Corrections Corporation 4152 Blue Heron Boulevard West Suite 108 Riviera Beach, FL 33406 Dear Mr. Roberts: This responds to your letter dated December 8, 1989, as supplemented by your two letters dated January 3 and 13, 1990, requesting an advisory opinion on behalf of Digital Corrections Corporation ("DCC") concerning application of the Federal Election Campaign Act of 1971, as amended ("the Act"), and Commission regulations to the use of 900 line telephone services to promote candidates and political committees. You state that DCC, a Florida corporation, is engaged in providing "900/700 inbound telephone services" to various entities. Such services allow individuals to dial into a digitalized voice program for a fee included on the caller's phone bill. The fee is set by the entities for whom the service is provided. You state that the appropriate local and national circuits are provided pursuant to agreements with AT&T and Cable & Wire, Inc. Communications, Inc. You wish to make 900 line telephone services available to candidates for Federal offices or to political committees that financially support their campaigns. Unlike the "listen only" capability provided by a number of companies providing POU line service, your service allows the caller to register a response by pressing buttons on a tonepad or by use of a voice response with a rotary phone. Each program will entail a voice message to the caller and a statement as to the cost of each call. Each caller will have the opportunity to hang up and incur no charge, and those callers remaining on the line will be informed that a receipt will be forwarded within thirty days for the cost of the call. The price per call will be fixed for each campaign with a range of $5 to $50. There will be no additional contribution added on to the fixed amount. You indicate that DCC is able to identify callers to ensure proper recordkeeping and to avoid corporate contributions. A feature used by DCC known as Automatic Number Identification identifies the exact telephone number from which the call was placed. The caller will be requested to provide name, address, and credit security number, and, upon completion of the call, DCC can cross-reference the call to identify the person in whose name the phone number is listed. DCC would then generate receipts to be sent to the caller and provide detailed summary information to the appropriate committee or candidate: including the caller's name, address, phone number, and total individual contributions per caller. DCC is able to identify the caller further through the use of voice certification. The voice can be digitally recorded and stored, thus enabling caller verification. You state that there will be billing only for calls placed from verified, "listed" residential telephones and that all other calls are "unrecorded." Thus, there will be no billing for a call if the name and address of the caller do not match
embedding.first_500_words: SENT BY:FACSIMILE AT 2/1/90, RECEIVED 2/1/90; 12:32 8:58 PM; 3-0 SGA 2 FEDERAL ELECTION COMMISSION WASHINGTON DC 20463 March 1, 1990 CERTIFIED MAIL RETURN RECEIPT REQUESTED ADVISORY OPINION 1990-1 Scott D. Roberts, President and Chief Executive Officer Digital Corrections Corporation 4152 Blue Heron Boulevard West Suite 108 Riviera Beach, FL 33404 Dear Mr. Roberts: This responds to your letter dated December 8, 1989, as supplemented by your letters dated January 3 and 5, 1990, requesting an advisory opinion on behalf of Digital Corrections Corporation ("DCC") concerning application of the Federal Election Campaign Act of 1971, as amended ("the Act"), and Commission regulations to the use of 900 line telephone services to promote candidates and political committees. You state that DCC, a Florida corporation, is engaged in providing "900/700 inbound telephone services" to various entities. Such services allow individuals to dial into an organization after receiving the program or a line included on the caller's phone bill. The fee is set by the entities for whom the service is provided. You note that the appropriate local and national circuit are provided pursuant to agreements with AT&T and Cable & Wireless Communications, Inc. You wish to make 900 line telephone services available to candidates for Federal offices or to political committees that financially support their campaigns. Unlike the "listener only" capability provided by a number of [illegible] We note that we have not indicated that any 1992 or other Presidential campaign may prohibit to contract with DCC, this advisory opinion does not address the separate and additional issues raised by 26 U.S.C. § [illegible] and related Commission regulations at 11 CFR Parts 9031-9039. AO 1950-1 Page 2 of companies providing PCI Line service, your service allows the caller to register a response by pressing buttons on a touch tone telephone in an even response with a rotary phone. Each program will entail a voice message to the caller and a statement as to the number of calls. Each call will have the opportunity to hang up and their no charge, and those callers remaining on the line will be informed that a receipt will be forwarded within thirty days for the cost of the call. The price per call will be fixed for each campaign with a range of $6 to $50. There will be no additional contribution added on to the billed amount. You indicate that DCC is able to identify callers to ensure proper recordkeeping and to avoid corporate contributions. A feature used by DCC known as Automatic Number Identification identifies the exact telephone number from which the call was placed. The caller will be requested to provide name, address, and mutual security number, and, upon completion of the call, DCC can cross-reference the call to identify the person in which name the phone number is listed. DCC would then continue assigns to be sent to the caller and provide detailed number information to the appropriate committee or candidate, including the caller's name, address, phone number, and total individual contribution
